notes: This is a Monday planning ceremony with two halves: a practice improvement catalog (converging moderately) and a KR review (converging weakly). The first half generates genuine distributed contributions — Francisco, Will, Michael, Yongmin, Yumi, and Vish all volunteer practice improvement items. Sam catalogs them and the team builds a shared agenda for the onsite week. The second half is a KR walkthrough that surfaces productive refinement (Francisco pushing for falsifiable KRs, Sam reinforcing quantitative standards) but runs out of time before achieving deep alignment. The meeting converges on WHAT to work on this week but not deeply on HOW, which is explicitly deferred to the onsite. Convergence is real but shallow. | Caveats: This meeting sits between the failed-feature retro (Transcript 003, Mar 6) and the onsite sessions (Transcripts 001/002, Mar 10). It functions as a bridge: collecting improvement intentions from the retro and staging them for the onsite. Convergence is directional (everyone agrees we need to improve practices) but not specific (the how is deferred).; The KR review portion is rushed. Multiple KRs get 30-second treatment. The Francisco-Sam exchange on falsifiable KRs is the only deep engagement. Other KR owners mostly report status without generating alignment discussion.; Sam is the primary organizer, cataloger, and synthesizer. The meeting's convergence depends heavily on his facilitation. If Sam were absent, it is unclear whether the team would self-organize to the same outcome.; The 0.75 confidence reflects that this is converging but weakly. The practice catalog is a genuine distributed contribution (strong signal). The KR review is mostly procedural (weak signal). The meeting achieves alignment on priorities but not on execution specifics.; Vish's Series A KR suggestion being rejected is a minor divergence. In a healthy team, rejected bids are fine as long as they are handled respectfully, which this one is. But it does mean the meeting's convergence is partially convergence-by-exclusion rather than convergence-by-integration.
